insights: Customer Support has the fastest median expense processing time (42.0 days), but this 22.5-day advantage over Finance (64.5 days) is not driven by transferable operational practices—user-level analysis reveals no consistent efficiency patterns, high intra-user variability (1–177 days), and no evidence of specialization or workload distribution advantages.

The speed advantage of Customer Support is strongly associated with case mix: it handles 28.0 percentage points more Africa-located cases, 17.0 points more One-time-type expenses, and 14.9 points more Assets-category expenses than other departments—each of these case types is independently linked to faster processing in prior analyses.

Customer Support’s workload is *more* concentrated—not less—with its top 3 users handling 31.6% of processed records versus 15.5% across other departments, contradicting the hypothesis that distributed expertise or balanced assignment drives speed.

Sales department processes expenses nearly as quickly as Customer Support (43.0 days median), indicating the speed advantage is not unique to one team and further undermining claims of department-specific operational excellence.

Within-department variance in processing times is extremely high (std 50–80 days), and the 22.5-day inter-department gap is only 0.34× the average within-department standard deviation—suggesting observed departmental differences are statistically modest relative to natural process noise.

Only 19 processed records exist for Customer Support versus 103 across all other departments combined, limiting statistical power and reinforcing that observed speed differences reflect small-sample case composition rather than systemic operational capability.
summary: The investigation confirms Customer Support processes expenses fastest (median 42.0 days), but finds no evidence that its speed stems from replicable operational practices—user-level analysis shows no specialization, high processing-time variability (1–177 days), and greater—not lesser—workload concentration. Instead, the advantage is attributable to a distinct case mix: significantly higher proportions of Africa-located, One-time-type, and Assets-category expenses, all previously associated with faster resolution. The near-identical speed of Sales (43.0 days) and the modest magnitude of the departmental gap relative to within-department variation further support that structural case characteristics—not transferable workflows—explain the difference. Given the lack of actionable operational levers, efforts to improve cross-departmental efficiency should prioritize case-mix-aware benchmarking and targeted interventions for slower-processing categories (e.g., recurring, non-Assets, non-Africa) rather than attempting to replicate Customer Support’s 'practices'.
